records.ety: [AS. hwītel, from hwit white; akin to Icel. hvītill a white bed cover. See White.]

defs: A grayish, coarse double blanket worn by countrywomen, in the west of England, over the shoulders, like a cloak or shawl.

records.ety: [OE. thwitel, fr. AS. pwītan to cut. Cf. Thwittle, Thwaite a piece of ground.]

defs: A knife; esp., a pocket, sheath, or clasp knife.
quotes: He wore a Sheffield whittle in his hose.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Whittled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Whittling (?).]

defs: To pare or cut off the surface of with a small knife; to cut or shape, as a piece of wood held in the hand, with a clasp knife or pocketknife.
To edge; to sharpen; to render eager or excited; esp., to excite with liquor; to inebriate.
quotes: “In vino veritas.” When men are well whittled, their tongues run at random.

defs: To cut or shape a piece of wood with am small knife; to cut up a piece of wood with a knife.
quotes: Dexterity with a pocketknife is a part of a Nantucket education; but I am inclined to think the propensity is national. Americans must and will whittle.
